did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier to diagnose melanoma from dermoscopy images. The method involves image pre-processing (Dull-Razor algorithm, median filter), segmentation (Chan-Vese method/active contour), and feature extraction (ABCD rule-based features including area and perimeter), which are then fed into the ANN model for classification, achieving 98% overall accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new automated diagnosis system for melanoma using dermoscopy images. It details a specific methodology involving image pre-processing (Dull-Razor, median filter), segmentation (Chan-Vese method), feature extraction (ABCD rule-based), and classification using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). The authors report original results, including a 98% overall accuracy achieved on a dataset of 200 images from the PH2 database, and compare their performance with other works. Although published in the proceedings of the 2019 5th International Conference on Advances in Electrical Engineering (ICAEE), it contains original experimental results rather than being a narrative review.

title: Melanoma Diagnosis from Dermoscopy Images Using Artificial Neural Network
journal: Proceedings of the 2019 5th International Conference on Advances in Electrical Engineering (ICAEE)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated system for diagnosing melanoma from dermoscopy images to avoid invasive biopsies. The methodology involves pre-processing with the Dull-Razor algorithm and median filter, segmentation using the Chan-Vese active contour method, and feature extraction based on the ABCD rule. The extracted features are classified using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) trained on 200 images from the PH2 database. The system achieved an overall accuracy of 98%, with 95% sensitivity and 98.75% specificity on the test set. The results suggest the approach can assist dermatologists in clinical decision-making, though the authors note the limitation of the small dataset size.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The study used 40 melanoma lesion images from the PH2 database, confirmed by histopathological test. The ANN output '1' specifies the malignant class.

The comparator class for the binary classification task. The study used 160 benign images from the PH2 database. The ANN output '0' specifies the benign class. The paper distinguishes these from melanoma based on symmetry, border, color, and diameter features.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label (Melanoma vs. Benign Melanocytic Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier to predict discrete diagnostic categories. The output layer has one neuron with output defined as '0' for the benign class and '1' for the malignant class. The task is binary classification based on ABCD rule features extracted from dermoscopy images.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image database used for training and testing the ANN classifier. The images were obtained from this database for the study.

use.training: Used to train the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier with 200 images (160 benign, 40 melanoma).
use.testing: The paper states the system was tested on two images collected from the internet, not from the PH2 database. However, the performance metrics (98% accuracy, 95% sensitivity, 98.75% specificity) are reported for the approach, likely derived from the PH2 dataset or a split thereof, though the specific split for these metrics is not explicitly defined beyond the total 200 images used for training/development.

datasets_used.name: Internet-collected Images
datasets_used.description: Two dermoscopy images (one benign, one melanoma) collected from the internet for external testing of the developed system.

use.testing: Used for external testing to demonstrate the system's ability to diagnose images not belonging to the original database.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics without statistical significance analysis.

PH2 Database.notes: The ANN classifier was trained on 200 images (160 benign, 40 melanoma) from the PH2 database. The confusion matrix for the PH2 dataset showed: True Positives (tp) = 38, False Negatives (fn) = 2 for Melanoma; False Positives (fp) = 2, True Negatives (tn) = 158 for Benign. The paper notes that diagnosis accuracy of 75-84% was reported by skilled dermatologists in reference [16].

Internet-collected Images.notes: Two images (one benign, one melanoma) collected from the internet were used for external testing. The system successfully diagnosed both images correctly, demonstrating the ability to diagnose images not belonging to the original database.

Comparative Literature.notes: The proposed method's accuracy is compared with other relevant works in Table III. The paper states it is difficult to compare directly because the considered datasets are different, but the proposed approach gives a promising result.

architecture_used.input_size: 8 features (A1, A2, B1, B2, B3, C, D1, D2)

training_details.loss_function: Error minimization (weights and biases updated to minimize error)

preprocessing_techniques: Dull-Razor algorithm for black hair removal
Chan-Vese method (active contour segmentation) for lesion segmentation
Conversion of grayscale image to binary image for segmentation
conclusions: The proposed automated diagnosis system using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with ABCD rule-based features achieved an overall accuracy of 98%, with 95% sensitivity and 98.75% specificity on the PH2 database.
The system successfully diagnosed two external test images (one benign, one melanoma) collected from the internet, demonstrating its ability to classify images outside the original training dataset.
The approach shows promise in assisting dermatologists in clinical decision-making by providing a non-invasive alternative to skin biopsy for melanoma detection.

The study acknowledges that direct comparison with other works is difficult due to differences in datasets, but notes that the proposed method's accuracy (98%) is higher than several recent studies (ranging from 93.24% to 94%).
The authors note the limitation of the small dataset size (200 images) used for training and testing.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 200 images (160 benign, 40 melanoma) from the PH2 database, which the authors explicitly acknowledge as a limitation.

The paper does not report a standard train/validation/test split for the PH2 dataset; it states 200 images were used for training, but reports performance metrics (98% accuracy) without clarifying if this was on a held-out test set or via cross-validation, raising potential data leakage or overfitting concerns.

External validation was performed on only two images collected from the internet (one benign, one melanoma), which is insufficient to demonstrate robust generalizability to unseen populations or devices.

The comparison with other works (e.g., 93-94% accuracy) is noted by the authors as difficult due to different datasets, making the claim of superior performance (98%) potentially misleading without standardized benchmarking.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.
The system uses handcrafted features based on the ABCD rule rather than deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex, non-linear patterns compared to modern CNN-based approaches.
